title: Textured wall tile in paper clay
subtitle: A relief-decorated clay tile with a hanging hole
excerpt: A paper clay wall tile is a flat slab with impressed or carved decoration, pierced for hanging, and sealed once bone-dry. Paper clay is stronger than standard air-dry clay at thin sections, so it holds fine impressed detail without cracking at the edges. The tile can be painted with acrylics or left in its natural pale finish. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Slab tile technique. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dry clay reinforced with cellulose fibres. Holds fine impressed detail better than standard air-dry clay and is less prone to edge cracking.
Fully air-dried clay. For air-dry paper clay, this is the finished state before sealing.

content.text: is better than standard air-dry clay for a wall tile because the fibre reinforcement keeps sharp impressed edges intact as the tile dries and reduces the warping that is common with large flat slabs. The result is a tile that takes fine detail well and lies flat on the wall. No kiln or wheel is needed.

attrs.title: Drying flat
attrs.body: Paper clay slabs warp less than standard air-dry clay, but drying between two flat boards still helps. Lay the freshly made tile on a canvas-covered board, place a second board on top, and weight lightly. Remove the top board after two hours and allow to finish drying in open air.

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 6 mm guide rails
Stamps, leaves, or lace for texture

content.text: Step 1: Roll and cut the slab

Roll the paper clay to 6 mm thick. Cut the tile to the shape and size you want. A 15 x 15 cm square is a practical starting size. Paper clay cuts cleanly with a fettling knife: press straight down with the blade against a ruler rather than dragging the knife, which pulls the edge.

Immediately after cutting, while the clay is still soft, press decoration into the surface. Press a leaf face-down with firm even pressure for a veined relief. Lay lace flat and roll once with the rolling pin. Press a stamp with a single firm push without rocking. Remove all objects before the clay begins to stiffen. Paper clay holds impressions slightly more crisply than standard air-dry clay because the fibres hold the walls of the impression open as the clay firms.

Use the needle tool to pierce two holes near the top of the tile, 3 cm apart and 1.5 cm from the top edge. Pierce straight through in one firm movement. Make the holes 2 to 3 mm wider than the cord diameter to allow for slight shrinkage as the clay dries.
Dry between two boards as described above, then allow to reach
in open air over 24 to 48 hours. Lightly sand rough edges with 120-grit sandpaper. Seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 with water. Thread cord through the hanging holes and tie at the back.
Paint with acrylic paint in two tones before sealing: a base colour, then a dry-brushed highlight over the raised texture.
Make a set of three tiles with different plant impressions, leaf, fern, grass, for a botanical grouping.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Issues when making the paper clay wall tile.

items.symptom: Tile still warps despite drying between boards
items.fix: Keep the top board on for the first four hours, not just two. If the tile has already warped, mist the concave surface lightly, re-press between boards, and weight more firmly.

items.symptom: Impressed detail is blurry at the edges
items.cause: Clay too wet when impressed, causing the walls to slump.
items.fix: Allow the slab to firm for 15 to 20 minutes after rolling before impressing. At this stage the clay takes a sharper impression without deforming.
